[Automobiles](/wiki/Automotive_industry "Automotive industry") and automotive parts (11%), [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") (9%), electric appliances and components (8%), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Thailand "Tourism in Thailand") (6%), [cement](/wiki/Cement "Cement"), auto [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"), [heavy](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry") and [light industries](/wiki/Light_industry "Light industry"), [appliances](/wiki/Home_appliance "Home appliance"), [computers](/wiki/Computer "Computer") and parts, [furniture](/wiki/Furniture "Furniture"), [plastics](/wiki/Plastics "Plastics"), [textiles](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles") and [garments](/wiki/Garments "Garments"), agricultural processing, [beverages](/wiki/Beverages "Beverages"), [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco")

[machinery](/wiki/Machinery "Machinery") (23%), [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") (19%), [foods](/wiki/Food "Food") and wood (14%), [chemicals](/wiki/Chemicals "Chemicals") and plastics (14%), [automobiles](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") and automotive parts (12%), stone and glass (7%), textiles and furniture (4%)

is dependent on exports, which accounted for about 58 percent of the country's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) in 2021.[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") itself is a [newly industrialized country](/wiki/Newly_industrialized_country "Newly industrialized country"), with a GDP of 17.922 trillion [baht](/wiki/Thai_baht "Thai baht") (US$514.8 billion) in 2023, the 9th largest economy in Asia.[[28]](#cite_note-:52-28) As of 2018, Thailand has an average [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") of 1.06%[[29]](#cite_note-Bank_of_Thailand-29) and an account surplus of 7.5% of the country's GDP.[[30]](#cite_note-nesdb.go.th-30) Its currency, the baht, is ranked as the tenth most frequently used world payment currency in 2017.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The **economy of Thailand** is dependent on exports, which accounted for about 58 percent of the country's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) in 2021.[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") itself is a [newly industrialized country](/wiki/Newly_industrialized_country "Newly industrialized country"), with a GDP of 17.922 trillion [baht](/wiki/Thai_baht "Thai baht") (US$514.8 billion) in 2023, the 9th largest economy in Asia.[[28]](#cite_note-:52-28) As of 2018, Thailand has an average [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") of 1.06%[[29]](#cite_note-Bank_of_Thailand-29) and an account surplus of 7.5% of the country's GDP.[[30]](#cite_note-nesdb.go.th-30) Its currency, the baht, is ranked as the tenth most frequently used world payment currency in 2017.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The industrial and [service sectors](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") are the main sectors in the Thai gross domestic product, with the former accounting for 39.2 percent of GDP. Thailand's [agricultural sector](/wiki/Agricultural_sector "Agricultural sector") produces 8.4 percent of GDP—lower than the trade and logistics and communication sectors, which account for 13.4 percent and 9.8 percent of GDP respectively. The construction and mining sectors add 4.3 percent to the country's gross domestic product. Other service sectors (including the financial, education, and hotel and restaurant sectors) account for 24.9 percent of the country's GDP.[[8]](#cite_note-Thailand_at_a_glance-8) Telecommunications and [trade in services](/wiki/Trade_in_services "Trade in services") are emerging as centers of industrial expansion and economic competitiveness.[[32]](#cite_note-WB-20080101-32)

Thailand is the second-largest economy in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), after [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). Its per capita GDP 255,362 baht (US$7,336) in 2023[[28]](#cite_note-:52-28) ranks fourth in Southeast Asian per capita GDP, after [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). In July 2018, Thailand held US$237.5 billion in international reserves,[[33]](#cite_note-bot.or.th-33) the second-largest in Southeast Asia (after Singapore). Its surplus in the [current account balance](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_current_account_balance "List of countries by current account balance") ranks tenth of the world, made US$37.898 billion to the country in 2018.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Thailand ranks second in Southeast Asia in external trade volume, after Singapore.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

The nation is recognized by the World Bank as "one of the great development success stories" in social and development indicators.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Despite a per capita gross national income (GNI) of US$7,090[[37]](#cite_note-37) and ranking 66th in the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI), the percentage of people below the national poverty line decreased from 65.26 percent in 1988 to 8.61 percent in 2016, according to the [Office of the National Economic and Social Development Council](/wiki/Office_of_the_National_Economic_and_Social_Development_Council "Office of the National Economic and Social Development Council")'s (NESDC) new poverty baseline.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Thailand is one of the [countries with the lowest unemployment rates](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_unemployment_rate "List of countries by unemployment rate") in the world, reported as one percent for the first quarter of 2014. This is due to a large proportion of the population working in subsistence agriculture or on other vulnerable employment (own-account work and unpaid family work).[[39]](#cite_note-ILO-39)

## Kingdom of Thailand budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Kingdom of Thailand budget")]

The [Kingdom of Thailand](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Thailand "Kingdom of Thailand")'s [FY2017](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") budget was 2.733 trillion [baht](/wiki/Thai_baht "Thai baht").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

In May 2018, the Thai Cabinet approved a FY2019 budget of three trillion baht, up 3.4 percent—100 billion baht—from FY2018. Annual revenue is projected to reach 2.55 trillion baht, up 4.1 percent, or 100 billion baht. Overall, the national budget will face a deficit of 450 billion baht. The cabinet also approved a budget deficit until 2022 in order to drive the economy to a growth of 3.5–4.5 percent a year.[[41]](#cite_note-Nation-20180529-41)

### Ayutthaya era (1351–1767)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ayutthaya era (1351–1767)")]

Thailand, formerly known as [Siam](/wiki/History_of_Thailand "History of Thailand"), opened to foreign contact in the [pre-industrial](/wiki/Pre-industrial_societies "Pre-industrial societies") era. The diverse natural resources of Siam were underutilized. However, coastal ports and cities at the river mouths were early economic centers which welcomed [merchants](/wiki/Merchant "Merchant") from [Persia](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Arab countries](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), Chinese dynasties and various kingdoms in [South](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). The rise of [Ayutthaya](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") during the 14th century was connected to renewed [maritime trade](/wiki/Maritime_transport "Maritime transport") with China, India and Southeast Asia. Ayutthaya became one of the most prosperous trade centers in Asia. However, the [Burmese–Siamese War (1765–1767)](/wiki/Burmese%E2%80%93Siamese_War_(1765%E2%80%931767) "Burmese–Siamese War (1765–1767)") caused immense economic harm with trade disruption and the eventual destruction of Ayutthaya in 1767.

### Rattanakosin era (1782–1932)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rattanakosin era (1782–1932)")]

When the capital of the kingdom moved to Bangkok during the 19th century, foreign trade (particularly with China) became the focus of the government. Chinese merchants came to trade; some settled in the country and received official positions. A number of Chinese merchants and migrants became high dignitaries in the court.

From the mid-19th century onward, European merchants were increasingly active. The [Bowring Treaty](/wiki/Bowring_Treaty "Bowring Treaty"), signed in 1855, guaranteed the privileges of British traders. The [Harris Treaty of 1856](/wiki/Townsend_Harris#Harris_Treaty_of_1856_with_Siam "Townsend Harris"), which updated the [Roberts Treaty](/wiki/Siamese-American_Treaty_of_Amity_and_Commerce "Siamese-American Treaty of Amity and Commerce") of 1833, extended the same guarantees to American traders. There were multiple costly conflicts with [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina") from the 18th till the mid 20th century. Siam lost major disputed territories such as [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") and [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") to the French colonists. The [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") gained control over Siamese provinces in the [Malay peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") and on the western border with [British Burma](/wiki/British_rule_in_Burma "British rule in Burma"). Nevertheless, Thailand was the only Southeast Asian country that remained sovereign from [European colonial powers](/wiki/European_colonial_powers "European colonial powers").

The domestic market developed slowly, with serfdom, [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_Thailand "Slavery in Thailand") related to the [sakdina](/wiki/Sakdina "Sakdina") system as major causes of domestic stagnation. Most of the male population in Siam was in the service of court officials, while their wives and daughters may have traded on a small scale in local markets. Those who were heavily indebted might sell themselves as slaves. King [Rama V](/wiki/Rama_V "Rama V") abolished serfdom and slavery in 1901 and 1905, respectively.

Thailand was a member of the [Allies of World War I](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"). From the early 20th century to the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Siam's economy gradually became globalized. Major entrepreneurs were ethnic Chinese who became Siamese nationals. Exports of agricultural products (especially rice) were very important and Thailand has been among the top rice exporters in the world. The Siamese economy suffered greatly from the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), a cause of the [Siamese revolution of 1932](/wiki/Siamese_revolution_of_1932 "Siamese revolution of 1932").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Significant investment in education in the 1930s (and again in the 1950s) laid the basis for economic growth, as did a liberal approach to trade and investment.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Bhumibol era (1946–2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bhumibol era (1946–2016)")]

#### Post-war era (1945–1955)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-war era (1945–1955)")]

Postwar domestic and international politics played significant roles in Thai economic development for most of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") era. From 1945 to 1947 (when the Cold War had not yet begun), the Thai economy suffered because of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). During the war, the Thai government (led by Field Marshal [Plaek Phibunsongkram](/wiki/Plaek_Phibunsongkhram "Plaek Phibunsongkhram")) allied with [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and declared war against the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"). After the war, Thailand had to supply 1.5 million tons of rice to Western countries without charge, a burden on the country's economic recovery. The government tried to solve the problem by establishing a rice office to oversee the rice trade. During this period, a multiple-exchange-rate system was introduced amid fiscal problems, and the kingdom experienced a shortage of consumer goods.[[44]](#cite_note-Nilnopkoon-44)

In November 1947, a brief democratic period was ended by a military coup and the Thai economy regained its momentum. In his dissertation, Somsak Nilnopkoon considers the period from 1947 to 1951 one of prosperity.[[44]](#cite_note-Nilnopkoon-44) By April 1948, Phibunsongkram, the wartime prime minister, returned to his previous office. However, he was caught in a power struggle between his subordinates. To preserve his power, Phibunsongkram began an anti-communist campaign to seek support from the United States.[[45]](#cite_note-สถิตนิรามัย-45) As a result, from 1950 onward, Thailand received military and economic aid from the US. The Phibunsongkram government established many state enterprises, which were seen as economic nationalism. The state (and its bureaucrats) dominated capital allocation in the kingdom. [Ammar Siamwalla](/wiki/Ammar_Siamwalla "Ammar Siamwalla"), one of Thailand's most prominent economists, calls it the period of "bureaucratic capitalism".[[45]](#cite_note-สถิตนิรามัย-45)

#### Power struggle and reform (1955–1985)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Power struggle and reform (1955–1985)")]

In 1955, Thailand began to see a change in its economy fueled by domestic and international politics. The power struggle between the two main factions of the Phibun regime—led by Police General [Phao Sriyanond](/wiki/Phao_Sriyanond "Phao Sriyanond") and General (later, Field Marshal) [Sarit Thanarat](/wiki/Sarit_Thanarat "Sarit Thanarat")—increased, causing Sriyanond to unsuccessfully seek support from the US for a coup against Phibunsongkram regime. Plaek Phibunsongkram attempted to democratize his regime, seeking popular support by developing the economy. He again turned to the US, asking for economic rather than military aid. The US responded with unprecedented economic aid to the kingdom from 1955 to 1959.[[45]](#cite_note-สถิตนิรามัย-45) The Phibunsongkram government also made important changes to the country's fiscal policies, including scrapping the multiple-exchange-rate system in favor of a fixed, unified system which was in use until 1984. The government also neutralized trade and conducted secret diplomacy with the People's Republic of China (PRC), displeasing the United States.

Despite his attempts to maintain power, Plaek Phibunsongkram was deposed (with Field Marshal [Phin Choonhavan](/wiki/Phin_Choonhavan "Phin Choonhavan") and Police General [Phao Siyanon](/wiki/Phao_Siyanon "Phao Siyanon")) on 16 September 1957 in a coup led by Field Marshal [Sarit Thanarat](/wiki/Sarit_Thanarat "Sarit Thanarat"). The Thanarat regime (in power from 1957 to 1973) maintained the course set by the Phibunsongkram regime with US support after severing all ties with the [PRC](/wiki/China "China") and supporting US operations in [Mainland Southeast Asia](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia") and the ongoing [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") (1955-1975). It developed the country's infrastructure and privatized state enterprises unrelated to that infrastructure. During this period, a number of economic institutions were established, including the Bureau of Budget, the [NESDC](/wiki/Office_of_the_National_Economic_and_Social_Development_Council "Office of the National Economic and Social Development Council"), and the [Thailand Board of Investment](/wiki/Thailand_Board_of_Investment "Thailand Board of Investment") (BOI). The National Economic and Social Development Plan was implemented in 1961.[[46]](#cite_note-46) During this period, the market-oriented Import-Substituting Industrialization (ISI) led to economic expansion in the kingdom during the 1960s.[[47]](#cite_note-47) According to former President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon")'s 1967 *Foreign Affairs* article, Thailand entered a period of rapid growth in 1958 (with an average growth rate of 7 percent per year).[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Economic problems (1970–1984)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Economic problems (1970–1984)")]

From the 1970s to 1984, Thailand suffered from many economic problems: decreasing US investment, budget deficits, oil-price spikes, and inflation. Domestic politics were also unstable. With the [Vietnamese occupation of Cambodia](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Kampuchea "People's Republic of Kampuchea") on 25 December 1978, Thailand became the front-line state in the struggle against communism, surrounded by two communist countries and a socialist Burma under General [Ne Win](/wiki/Ne_Win "Ne Win"). Successive governments tried to solve the economic problems by promoting exports and tourism, still important for the Thai economy.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

One of the best-known measures to deal with the economic problems of that time was implemented under General [Prem Tinsulanonda](/wiki/Prem_Tinsulanonda "Prem Tinsulanonda")'s government, in power from 1980 to 1988. Between 1981 and 1984, the government devalued the national currency, the [Thai baht](/wiki/Thai_baht "Thai baht") (THB), three times. On 12 May 1981, it was devalued by 1.07 percent, from THB20.775/US$ to THB21/US$. On 15 July 1981, it was again devalued, this time by 8.7 percent (from THB21/US$ to THB23/US$). The third devaluation, on 5 November 1984, was the most significant: 15 percent, from THB23/US$ to THB27/US$.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The government also replaced the country's fixed exchange rate (where it was pegged to the US dollar) with a "multiple currency basket peg system" in which the US dollar bore 80 percent of the weight.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Calculated from the [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund")'s World Economic Outlook Database, in the period 1980–1984, the Thai economy had an average GDP growth rate of 5.4 percent.[[52]](#cite_note-WEOD-52)

#### Economic boom (1985–1997)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economic boom (1985–1997)")]

Concurrent with the third devaluation of the Thai baht, on 22 September 1985, Japan, the US, the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), and West Germany signed the [Plaza Accord](/wiki/Plaza_Accord "Plaza Accord") to depreciate the US dollar in relation to the yen and the [Deutsche Mark](/wiki/Deutsche_Mark "Deutsche Mark"). Since the dollar accounted for 80 percent of the Thai currency basket, the baht was depreciated further, making Thailand's exports more competitive and the country more attractive to foreign direct investment (FDI) (especially from Japan, whose currency had appreciated since 1985). In 1988, Prem Tinsulanonda resigned and was succeeded by [Chatichai Choonhavan](/wiki/Chatichai_Choonhavan "Chatichai Choonhavan"), the first democratically elected prime minister of Thailand since 1976. The [Cambodian-Vietnamese War](/wiki/Cambodian-Vietnamese_War "Cambodian-Vietnamese War") was ending; Vietnam gradually retreated from Cambodia by 1989, enhancing Thai economic development.

After the 1984 baht devaluation and the 1985 [Plaza Accord](/wiki/Plaza_Accord "Plaza Accord"), although the public sector struggled due to fiscal constraints, the private sector grew. The country's improved foreign trade and an influx of foreign direct investment (mainly from Japan) triggered an economic boom from 1987 to 1996. Although Thailand had previously promoted its exports, during this period the country shifted from [import-substitution](/wiki/Import_substitution_industrialization "Import substitution industrialization") (ISI) to [export-oriented industrialization](/wiki/Export-oriented_industrialization "Export-oriented industrialization") (EOI). During this decade the Thai GDP (calculated from the IMF World Economic Outlook database) had an average growth rate of 9.5 percent per year, with a peak of 13.3 percent in 1988.[[52]](#cite_note-WEOD-52) In the same period, the volume of Thai exports of goods and services had an average growth rate of 14.8 percent, with a peak of 26.1 percent in 1988.[[52]](#cite_note-WEOD-52)

Economic problems persisted. From 1987 to 1996, Thailand experienced a [current account](/wiki/Current_account_(balance_of_payments) "Current account (balance of payments)") deficit averaging −5.4 percent of GDP per year, and the deficit continued to increase. In 1996, the current account deficit accounted for −7.887 percent of GDP (US$14.351 billion).[[52]](#cite_note-WEOD-52) A shortage of capital was another problem. The first [Chuan Leekpai](/wiki/Chuan_Leekpai "Chuan Leekpai") government, in office from September 1992 to May 1995, tried to solve this problem by granting Bangkok [International Banking Facility](/wiki/International_Banking_Facility "International Banking Facility") (BIBF) licenses to Thai banks in 1993. This allowed BIBF banks to benefit from Thailand's high-interest rate by borrowing from foreign financial institutions at low interest and loaning to Thai businesses. By 1997, foreign debt had risen to US$109,276 billion (65% of which was short-term debt), while Thailand had US$38,700 billion in international reserves.[[53]](#cite_note-Chutipat-53) Many loans were backed by [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate"), creating an [economic bubble](/wiki/Economic_bubble "Economic bubble"). By late 1996, there was a loss of confidence in the country's financial institutions; the government closed 18 [trust companies](/wiki/Trust_company "Trust company") and three [commercial banks](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank"). The following year, 56 financial institutions were closed by the government.[[53]](#cite_note-Chutipat-53)

Another problem was foreign speculation. Aware of Thailand's economic problems and its [currency basket](/wiki/Currency_basket "Currency basket") exchange rate, foreign speculators (including [hedge funds](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund")) were certain that the government would again devalue the baht, under pressure on both the [spot](/wiki/Spot_market "Spot market") and [forward markets](/wiki/Forward_market "Forward market"). In the spot market, to force devaluation, speculators took out loans in baht and made loans in dollars. In the forward market, speculators (believing that the baht would soon be devalued) bet against the currency by contracting with dealers who would give dollars in return for an agreement to repay a specific amount of baht several months in the future.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/USD_to_Thai_Baht_exchange_rate.webp/330px-USD_to_Thai_Baht_exchange_rate.webp.png)](/wiki/File:USD_to_Thai_Baht_exchange_rate.webp)

[USD](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") / [Thai Baht](/wiki/Thai_baht "Thai baht") [exchange rate](/wiki/Exchange_rate "Exchange rate")

In the government, there was a call from Virapong Ramangkul (an economic advisor of Prime Minister [Chavalit Yongchaiyudh](/wiki/Chavalit_Yongchaiyudh "Chavalit Yongchaiyudh")) to devalue the baht, which was supported by former Prime Minister [Prem Tinsulanonda](/wiki/Prem_Tinsulanonda "Prem Tinsulanonda").[[55]](#cite_note-55) Yongchaiyudh ignored them, relying on the [Bank of Thailand](/wiki/Bank_of_Thailand "Bank of Thailand") (led by Governor Rerngchai Marakanond, who spent as much as US$24 billion – about two-thirds of Thailand's international reserves) to protect the baht. On 2 July 1997, Thailand had US$2,850 billion remaining in international reserves,[[53]](#cite_note-Chutipat-53) and could no longer protect the baht. That day Marakanond decided to [float](/wiki/Floating_exchange_rate "Floating exchange rate") the baht, triggering the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis").

#### Financial crisis (1997–2000)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Financial crisis (1997–2000)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Asian_Financial_Crisis_EN_vector.svg/250px-Asian_Financial_Crisis_EN_vector.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Asian_Financial_Crisis_EN_vector.svg)

Countries affected by the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis")

The Thai economy collapsed as a result of the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"). Within a few months, the value of the baht floated from THB25/US$ (its lowest point) to THB56/US$. The [Stock Exchange of Thailand (SET)](/wiki/Stock_Exchange_of_Thailand_(SET) "Stock Exchange of Thailand (SET)") dropped from a peak of 1,753.73 in 1994 to a low of 207.31 in 1998.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The country's GDP dropped from THB3.115 trillion at the end of 1996 to THB2.749 trillion at the end of 1998. In dollar terms, it took Thailand as long as 10 years to regain its 1996 GDP. The unemployment rate went up nearly threefold: from 1.5 percent of the labor force in 1996 to 4.4 percent in 1998.[[57]](#cite_note-Thailand-57)

A sharp decrease in the value of the baht abruptly increased foreign debt, undermining financial institutions. Many were sold, in part, to foreign investors while others went bankrupt. Due to low international reserves from the Bank of Thailand's currency-protection measures, the government had to accept a loan from the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF). Overall, Thailand received US$17.2 billion in [aid](/wiki/Aid "Aid").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The crisis impacted Thai politics. One direct effect was that Prime Minister [Chavalit Yongchaiyudh](/wiki/Chavalit_Yongchaiyudh "Chavalit Yongchaiyudh") resigned under pressure on 6 November 1997, succeeded by opposition leader [Chuan Leekpai](/wiki/Chuan_Leekpai "Chuan Leekpai"). The second Leekpai government, in office from November 1997 to February 2001, tried to implement economic reforms based on IMF-guided [neo-liberal capitalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism"). It pursued strict fiscal policies (keeping interest rates high and cutting government spending), enacting 11 laws it called "bitter medicine" and critics called "the 11 nation-selling laws". The Thai government and its supporters maintained that with these measures, the Thai economy improved.

In 1999, Thailand had a positive GDP growth rate for the first time since the crisis.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Many critics, however, mistrusted the IMF and maintained that government-spending cuts harmed the recovery. Unlike economic problems in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), they asserted, the Asian financial crisis was born in the private sector and the IMF measures were inappropriate. The positive growth rate in 1999 was because the country's GDP had gone down for two consecutive years, as much as −10.5 percent in 1998 alone. In terms of the baht, it was not until 2002 (in dollar terms, not until 2006) that Thailand would regain its 1996 GDP. An additional 1999 loan from the Miyazawa Plan made the question of whether (or to what extent) the Leekpai government helped the Thai economy controversial.

#### Early 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Early 21st century")]

An indirect effect of the financial crisis on Thai politics was the rise of [Thaksin Shinawatra](/wiki/Thaksin_Shinawatra "Thaksin Shinawatra"). In reaction to the government's economic policies, Thaksin Shinawatra's [Thai Rak Thai Party](/wiki/Thai_Rak_Thai_Party "Thai Rak Thai Party") won a landslide victory over Chuan Leekpai's [Democrat Party](/wiki/Democrat_Party_(Thailand) "Democrat Party (Thailand)") in the [2001 general election](/wiki/2001_Thai_general_election "2001 Thai general election") and took office in February 2001. Although weak export demand held the GDP growth rate to 2.2 percent in the first year of his administration, the first Thaksin Shinawatra government performed well from 2002 to 2004 with growth rates of 5.3, 7.1 and 6.3 percent respectively. His policy was later called [Thaksinomics](/wiki/Thaksinomics "Thaksinomics"). During Thaksin's first term, Thailand's economy regained momentum and the country paid off its IMF debt by July 2003 (two years ahead of schedule). Despite criticism of Thaksinomics, Thaksin's party won another landslide victory over the Democrat Party in the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_Thai_general_election "2005 Thai general election"). The official economic data related to Thaksinomics reveals that between 2001 and 2011, the GDP per capita of [Isan](/wiki/Isan "Isan"), the country's large north-eastern region, more than doubled to US$1,475, while, over the same period, GDP in the Bangkok area rose from US$7,900 to nearly US$13,000.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The cost of air and water pollution in Thailand is around 1.6–2.6% of GDP per year based on a 2004 indicator.[[61]](#cite_note-World_Bank-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Phuket_after_tsunami_%282004%29.jpg/250px-Phuket_after_tsunami_%282004%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phuket_after_tsunami_(2004).jpg)

[Phuket](/wiki/Phuket_(city) "Phuket (city)") in 2004. The Indian Ocean tsunami affected the economy

Thaksin's second term was less successful. The [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami") caused a big human toll, damage and negatively impacted the first-quarter Thai GDP in 2005. The [Yellow Shirts](/wiki/People%27s_Alliance_for_Democracy "People's Alliance for Democracy"), a coalition of protesters against Thaksin, also emerged in 2005. In 2006, Thaksin dissolved the parliament and called for a general election. The [April 2006 general election](/wiki/April_2006_Thai_general_election "April 2006 Thai general election") was boycotted by the main opposition parties. Thaksin's party won again, but the election was declared invalid by the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Thailand "Constitutional Court of Thailand"). Another [general election](/wiki/October_2006_Thai_general_election "October 2006 Thai general election"), scheduled for October 2006, was cancelled. On 19 September a group of soldiers calling themselves the [Council for Democratic Reform under the Constitutional Monarchy](/wiki/Council_for_National_Security "Council for National Security") led by [Sonthi Boonyaratglin](/wiki/Sonthi_Boonyaratglin "Sonthi Boonyaratglin") organized a coup, ousting Thaksin while he was in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") preparing for a speech at the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly"). During the last year of the second Thaksin government, the Thai GDP grew by 5.1 percent. Under his government, Thailand's overall ranking in the IMD Global Competitiveness Scoreboard rose from 31st in 2002 to 25th in 2005 before falling to 29th in 2006.[[62]](#cite_note-tma.or.th-62)

After the coup, Thailand's economy again suffered. From the last quarter of 2006 through 2007, the country was ruled by a military junta led by General [Surayud Chulanont](/wiki/Surayud_Chulanont "Surayud Chulanont"), who was appointed prime minister in October 2006. The 2006 GDP growth rate slowed from 6.1, 5.1 and 4.8 percent year-over-year in the first three-quarters to 4.4 percent (YoY) in Q4.[[63]](#cite_note-Quarterly_Gross_Domestic_Product-63) Thailand's ranking on the IMD Global Competitiveness Scoreboard fell from 26th in 2005 to 29th in 2006 and 33rd in 2007.[[62]](#cite_note-tma.or.th-62) Thaksin's plan for massive infrastructure investments was unmentioned until 2011, when his younger sister [Yingluck Shinawatra](/wiki/Yingluck_Shinawatra "Yingluck Shinawatra") entered office. In 2007, the Thai economy grew by 5 percent. On 23 December 2007, the military government held a [general election](/wiki/2007_Thai_general_election "2007 Thai general election"). The pro-Thaksin [People's Power Party](/wiki/People%27s_Power_Party_(Thailand) "People's Power Party (Thailand)"), led by [Samak Sundaravej](/wiki/Samak_Sundaravej "Samak Sundaravej"), won a landslide victory over [Abhisit Vejjajiva](/wiki/Abhisit_Vejjajiva "Abhisit Vejjajiva")'s Democrat Party.

Under the People's Power Party-led government the country fell into political turmoil. This, combined with the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") cut the 2008 Thai GDP growth rate to 2.5%.[[63]](#cite_note-Quarterly_Gross_Domestic_Product-63) Before the [People's Alliance for Democracy](/wiki/People%27s_Alliance_for_Democracy "People's Alliance for Democracy") (PAD) and the Yellow Shirts reconvened in March 2008, the GDP grew by 6.5 percent (YoY) in the first quarter of the year.[[63]](#cite_note-Quarterly_Gross_Domestic_Product-63) Thailand's ranking on the IMD World Competitiveness Scoreboard rose from 33rd in 2007 to 27th in 2008. The Yellow Shirts occupied the Government House of Thailand in August 2008, and on 9 September the Constitutional Court delivered a decision removing [Samak Sundaravej](/wiki/Samak_Sundaravej "Samak Sundaravej") from the [prime ministership](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Thailand "Prime Minister of Thailand").

[Somchai Wongsawat](/wiki/Somchai_Wongsawat "Somchai Wongsawat"), Thaksin's brother-in-law, succeeded Samak Sundaravej as prime minister on 18 September. In the US, the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") reached its peak while the Yellow Shirts were still in Government House, impeding government operations. GDP growth dropped from 5.2 percent (YoY) in Q2 2008 to 3.1 percent (YoY) and −4.1 percent (YoY) in Q3 and Q4. From 25 November to 3 December 2008 the Yellow Shirts, protesting Somchai Wongsawat's prime ministership, seized the two Bangkok airports, ([Suvarnabhumi](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi_Airport "Suvarnabhumi Airport") and [Don Mueang](/wiki/Don_Mueang_International_Airport "Don Mueang International Airport")), and damaged Thailand's image and economy. On 2 December the Thai Constitutional Court issued a decision dissolving the People's Power Party, ousting Somchai Wongsawat as prime minister.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/President_Lee_holds_summit_with_Thai_Prime_Minister_Abhisit_Vejjajiva_%284345441042%29.jpg/250px-President_Lee_holds_summit_with_Thai_Prime_Minister_Abhisit_Vejjajiva_%284345441042%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Lee_holds_summit_with_Thai_Prime_Minister_Abhisit_Vejjajiva_(4345441042).jpg)

A coalition government under [Abhisit Vejjajiva](/wiki/Abhisit_Vejjajiva "Abhisit Vejjajiva") was formed in 2008

By the end of 2008, a coalition government led by [Abhisit Vejjajiva](/wiki/Abhisit_Vejjajiva "Abhisit Vejjajiva")'s Democrat Party was formed: "[The] legitimacy of the Abhisit government has been questioned since the first day that the Democrat party took the office in 2008 as it was allegedly formed by the military in a military camp".[[64]](#cite_note-64) The government was under pressure from the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the [Red Shirts](/wiki/Red_Shirts_(Thailand) "Red Shirts (Thailand)"), who refused to acknowledge Abhisit Vejjajiva's prime ministry and called for new elections as soon as possible. However, Abhisit rejected the call until he dissolved the parliament for a new election in May 2011. In 2009, his first year in office, Thailand experienced a negative growth rate for the first time since the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"): a GDP of −2.3 percent.[[63]](#cite_note-Quarterly_Gross_Domestic_Product-63)

In 2010, the country's growth rate increased to 7.8 percent. However, with the instability surrounding the major 2010 protests, the GDP growth of Thailand settled at around 4–5 percent from highs of 5–7 percent under the previous civilian administration—political uncertainty was identified as the primary cause of a decline in investor and consumer confidence. The IMF predicted that the Thai economy would rebound strongly from the low 0.1 percent GDP growth in 2011, to 5.5 percent in 2012 and then 7.5 percent in 2013, due to the accommodating monetary policy of the Bank of Thailand, as well as a package of fiscal stimulus measures introduced by the incumbent [Yingluck Shinawatra](/wiki/Yingluck_Shinawatra "Yingluck Shinawatra") government.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

In the first two-quarters of 2011, when the political situation was relatively calm, the Thai GDP grew by 3.2 and 2.7 percent (YoY).[[63]](#cite_note-Quarterly_Gross_Domestic_Product-63) Under Abhisit's administration, Thailand's ranking fell from 26 in 2009, to 27 in 2010 and 2011,[[62]](#cite_note-tma.or.th-62) and the country's infrastructure declined since 2009.[[62]](#cite_note-tma.or.th-62)

In the [2011 general election](/wiki/2011_Thai_general_election "2011 Thai general election"), the pro-Thaksin [Pheu Thai Party](/wiki/Pheu_Thai_Party "Pheu Thai Party") again won a decisive victory over the Democrat Party, and Thaksin's youngest sister, [Yingluck Shinawatra](/wiki/Yingluck_Shinawatra "Yingluck Shinawatra"), succeeded Abhisit as prime minister. Elected in July, the Pheu Thai Party-led government began its administration in late-August, and when [Yingluck Shinawatra](/wiki/Yingluck_Shinawatra "Yingluck Shinawatra") entered office, the [2011 Thailand floods](/wiki/2011_Thailand_floods "2011 Thailand floods") threatened the country—from 25 July 2011 to 16 January 2012, flood waters covered 65 of the country's 76 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Thailand "Provinces of Thailand"). The World Bank assessed the total damage in December 2011 and reported a cost of THB1.425 trillion (about US$45.7 billion).[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The 2011 GDP growth rate fell to 0.1 percent, with a contraction of 8.9 percent (YoY) in Q4 alone.[[67]](#cite_note-Gross_Domestic_Product:_Q2/2012-67) The country's overall competitiveness ranking, according to the IMD World Competitiveness Scoreboard, fell from 27 in 2011 to 30 in 2012.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In 2012, Thailand was recovering from the previous year's severe flood. The Yingluck government planned to develop the country's infrastructure, ranging from a long-term water-management system to logistics. The [Euro area crisis](/wiki/Euro_area_crisis "Euro area crisis") reportedly harmed Thailand's economic growth in 2012, directly and indirectly affecting the country's exports. Thailand's GDP grew by 6.5 percent, with a headline inflation rate of 3.02 percent, and a current account surplus of 0.7 percent of the country's GDP.[[69]](#cite_note-http-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Baht2013_protest.jpg/250px-Baht2013_protest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baht2013_protest.jpg)

The Thai baht depreciated during the [2013 Thai protests](/wiki/2013%E2%80%932014_Thai_political_crisis "2013–2014 Thai political crisis")

On 23 December 2013, the Thai baht dropped to a three-year low due to the political unrest during the preceding months. According to *[Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_News "Bloomberg News")*, the Thai currency lost 4.6 percent over November and December, while the main stock index also dropped (9.1 percent).[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Following the Thai military coup in May 2014, [Agence France Presse](/wiki/Agence_France_Presse "Agence France Presse") (AFP) published an article claiming that the nation was on the "verge of recession". Published on 17 June 2014, the article's main subject is the departure of nearly 180,000 [Cambodians](/wiki/Demographics_of_Cambodia "Demographics of Cambodia") from Thailand due to fears of an immigration "clampdown", but concluded with information on the Thai economy's contraction of 2.1 percent quarter-on-quarter, from January to the end of March 2014.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Since the cessation of the curfew that was enacted by the military in May 2014, the Federation of Thai Industries (FTI)'s chairman, Supant Mongkolsuthree, said that he projects growth of 2.5–3 percent for the Thai economy in 2014, as well as a revitalisation of the [Thai tourism industry](/wiki/Tourism_in_Thailand "Tourism in Thailand") in the second half of 2014. Furthermore, Supant also cited the Board of Investment's future consideration of a backlog of investment projects, estimated at 700 billion baht, as an economically beneficial process that would occur around October 2014.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Thailand's flagging economic performance led, at the end of 2015, to increased criticism of the [National Council for Peace and Order](/wiki/National_Council_for_Peace_and_Order "National Council for Peace and Order")'s (NCPO) handling of the economy, both at home and in influential Western media.[[73]](#cite_note-Pesek-20151204-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) The country's economic growth of 2.8% in the first quarter of 2019 was recorded to be the slowest since 2014.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Vajiralongkorn era (2016–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Vajiralongkorn era (2016–present)")]

The military government unveiled its newest economic initiative, "Thailand 4.0", in 2016. Thailand 4.0 is the "...master plan to free Thailand from the [middle-income trap](/wiki/Middle_income_trap "Middle income trap"), making it a high-income nation in five years."[[76]](#cite_note-BP-20170102-76)

The government narrative describes Thailand 1.0 as the agrarian economy of Thailand decades ago. Thailand 1.0 gave way to Thailand 2.0, when the nation's economy moved on to light industry, textiles, and food processing. Thailand 3.0 describes the present day, with heavy industry and energy accounting for up to 70 percent of the Thai GDP.[[76]](#cite_note-BP-20170102-76) Thailand 4.0 is described as an economy driven by [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") industries and innovation that will lead to the production of value-added products and services. According to General [Prayut Chan-o-cha](/wiki/Prayut_Chan-o-cha "Prayut Chan-o-cha"), the prime minister, Thailand 4.0 is composed of three elements: 1. Make Thailand a high-income nation, 2. Make Thailand a more inclusive society, and 3. Focus on sustainable growth and development.[[77]](#cite_note-TBN-20160915-77)

Critics of Thailand 4.0 point out that Thailand lacks the specialists and experts, especially in high-technology, needed to modernise the Thai industry. "...the government will have to allow the import of foreign specialists to help bring forward Thailand 4.0," said Dr. Somchai Jitsuchon, research director for inclusive development at the [Thailand Development Research Institute](/wiki/Thailand_Development_Research_Institute "Thailand Development Research Institute") (TDRI). "...that won't be easy as local professional associations will oppose the idea as they want to reserve those professional careers for Thais only".[[76]](#cite_note-BP-20170102-76) He went on to point out that only 56 percent of Thailand's population has access to the Internet, an obstacle to the creation of a high-tech workforce. A major thrust of Thailand 4.0 is encouraging a move to [industrial robot](/wiki/Industrial_robot "Industrial robot") manufacturing. However, Thailand's membership in the [ASEAN Economic Community](/wiki/ASEAN_Economic_Community "ASEAN Economic Community") (AEC), makes cheap workers from neighbouring countries even more readily available, which will make it harder to make the economic case to switch to robots. Somchai also pointed out that the bureaucratic nature of the Thai government will make realisation of Thailand 4.0 difficult. Every action plan calls for results from several ministries, "all of which are big, clumsily-run organisations" slow to perform.[[76]](#cite_note-BP-20170102-76)

In September 2020, the [World Bank Group](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group") forecasted that the Thai economy would contract 8.9% by the end of the year due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Thailand "COVID-19 pandemic in Thailand").[[78]](#cite_note-78) The Thai government reduced the [jet fuel tax](/wiki/Jet_fuel_tax "Jet fuel tax") between April 2020 and June 2023.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)

In August 2024, the dismissal of Thai Prime Minister [Srettha Thavisin](/wiki/Srettha_Thavisin "Srettha Thavisin") exacerbated Thailand's economic difficulties, impeding the implementation of significant financial measures. This political upheaval intensified existing issues such as high [household debt](/wiki/Household_debt "Household debt") and economic stagnation, further undermining investor confidence and economic stability.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Over the past 32 years, the economy of Thailand has expanded. The GDP at current prices shows that from 1980 to 2012 the Thai economy has expanded nearly sixteen-fold when measured in baht, or nearly eleven-fold when measured in dollars. This makes Thailand the [32nd](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)")-biggest economy in the world, according to the IMF. With regard to GDP, Thailand has undergone five periods of economic growth. From 1980 to 1984, the economy has grown by an average of 5.4 percent per year. Regional businesses account for 70 percent of GDP, with Bangkok contributing 30 percent.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

After the 1984 baht devaluation and the 1985 Plaza Accord, a significant amount of foreign direct investment (mainly from Japan) raised the average growth rate per year to 8.8 percent from 1985 to 1996 before slumping to −5.9 percent per year from 1997 to 1998. From 1999 to 2006, Thailand averaged a growth rate of 5.0% per year. Since 2007, the country has faced a number of challenges: a military coup in late 2006, political turmoil from 2008 to 2011, the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), floods in 2010 and 2011, and the [Euro area crisis](/wiki/Euro_area_crisis "Euro area crisis"). As a result, from 2007 to 2012 the average GDP growth rate was 3.25% per year.

According to the IMF, in 2012 Thailand ranked [92nd](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita") in the world in its nominal GDP per capita.

## Poverty and inequality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Poverty and inequality")]

The number of Thailand's poor declined from 7.1 million people in 2014 (10.5 percent of the population), to 4.9 million people in 2015 (7.2 percent of the population). Thailand's 2014 poverty line was defined as an income of 2,647 baht per month. For 2015 it was 2,644 baht per month. According to the [NESDC](/wiki/Office_of_the_National_Economic_and_Social_Development_Council "Office of the National Economic and Social Development Council") in a report entitled, *Poverty and Inequality in Thailand*, the country's growth in 2014 was 0.8 percent and 2.8 percent in 2015. NESDC Secretary-General Porametee Vimolsiri said that the growth was due to the effect of governmental policies. The report also noted that 10 percent of the Thai population earned 35 percent of Thailand's aggregate income and owned 61.5 percent of its land.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Thailand was ranked as the world's [third most unequal nation](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_wealth_inequality "List of sovereign states by wealth inequality"), behind Russia and India, in the Credit Suisse *Global Wealth Databook 2016* (companion volume to the *Global Wealth Report 2016*[[85]](#cite_note-85)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]), with one percent of the Thai population estimated to own 58 percent of Thailand's wealth.[[86]](#cite_note-BP-20170630-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

## Industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Industries")]

### SMEs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: SMEs")]

Virtually all of Thailand's firms, 99.7 percent, or 2.7 million enterprises, are classified as [small or medium-sized enterprises](/wiki/Small_and_medium-sized_enterprises "Small and medium-sized enterprises") (SMEs). As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit), SMEs account for 80.3 percent (13 million) of Thailand's total employment. Despite the large number of SMEs, their contribution to the nation's GDP decreased from 41.3 percent of GDP in 2002 to 37.4 percent in 2013. Their declining contribution is reflected in their turnover rate: seventy percent fail within "...a few years".[[88]](#cite_note-WB-20161107-88): 47

### Agriculture, forestry and fishing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Agriculture, forestry and fishing")]

Main article: [Agriculture in Thailand](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Thailand "Agriculture in Thailand")

See also: [Rice production in Thailand](/wiki/Rice_production_in_Thailand "Rice production in Thailand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Rice_farmers_Mae_Wang_Chiang_Mai_Province.jpg/330px-Rice_farmers_Mae_Wang_Chiang_Mai_Province.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rice_farmers_Mae_Wang_Chiang_Mai_Province.jpg)

Thailand has been the [largest rice exporter in the world](/wiki/Rice_production_in_Thailand "Rice production in Thailand"). Forty-nine percent of Thailand's labor force is employed in agriculture.[[89]](#cite_note-ODI1-89)

Developments in agriculture since the 1960s have supported Thailand's transition to an industrialised economy.[[89]](#cite_note-ODI1-89) As recently as 1980, agriculture supplied 70 percent of employment.[[89]](#cite_note-ODI1-89) In 2008, agriculture, forestry and fishing contributed 8.4 percent to GDP; in rural areas, farm jobs supply half of employment.[[89]](#cite_note-ODI1-89) [Rice is the most important crop in the country](/wiki/Rice_production_in_Thailand "Rice production in Thailand") and Thailand had long been the world's number one exporter of rice, until recently falling behind both India and Vietnam.[[90]](#cite_note-90) It is a major exporter of shrimp. Other crops include coconuts, corn, rubber, soybeans, [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane") and [tapioca](/wiki/Tapioca "Tapioca").[[91]](#cite_note-91)

Thailand is the world's third-largest seafood exporter. Overall fish exports were worth around US$3 billion in 2014, according to the Thai Frozen Foods Association. Thailand's fishing industry employs more than 300,000 persons.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

In 1985, Thailand designated 25 percent of its land area for forest protection and 15 percent for timber production. Forests have been set aside for conservation and recreation, and timber forests are available for the forestry industry. Between 1992 and 2001, exports of logs and sawn timber increased from 50,000 to 2,000,000 cubic meters per year.

The regional [avian-flu](/wiki/Avian_influenza "Avian influenza") outbreak contracted Thailand's agricultural sector in 2004, and the tsunami of 26 December devastated the Andaman Sea fishing industry. In 2005 and 2006, agricultural GDP was reported to have contracted by 10 percent.[[93]](#cite_note-cp-93)

Thailand is the world's second-largest exporter of [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum") (after Canada), although government policy limits gypsum exports to support prices. In 2003 Thailand produced more than 40 different minerals, with an annual value of about US$740 million. In September 2003, to encourage foreign investment in mining the government relaxed its restrictions on mining by foreign companies and reduced mineral royalties owed to the state.[[93]](#cite_note-cp-93)

### Industry and manufacturing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Industry and manufacturing")]

[![Women wearing gloves and masks on an assembly line](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/SPS_Intertech_factory13.jpg/330px-SPS_Intertech_factory13.jpg)](/wiki/File:SPS_Intertech_factory13.jpg)

Production-line workers at a factory in [Chachoengsao](/wiki/Chachoengsao "Chachoengsao").

In 2007 industry contributed 43.9 percent of GDP, employing 14 percent of the workforce. Industry expanded at an average annual rate of 3.4 percent from 1995 to 2005. The most important sub-sector of industry is manufacturing, which accounted for 34.5 percent of GDP in 2004.

#### Electrical and electronics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Electrical and electronics")]

Electrical and electronics (E&E) equipment is Thailand's largest export sector, amounting to about 15 percent of total exports. In 2014 Thailand's E&E exports totaled US$55 billion.[[94]](#cite_note-ILO-201607-94): 28 The E&E sector employed approximately 780,000 workers in 2015, representing 12.2 per cent of the total employment in manufacturing.[[94]](#cite_note-ILO-201607-94): 27

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit), Thailand is the largest exporter of computers and computer components in ASEAN. Thailand is the world's second-biggest maker of [hard disk drives](/wiki/Hard_disk_drive "Hard disk drive") (HDDs) after China, with [Western Digital](/wiki/Western_Digital "Western Digital") and [Seagate Technology](/wiki/Seagate_Technology "Seagate Technology") among the biggest producers.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Reuters-20150318-96) But problems may loom for Thailand's high-tech sector. In January 2015, the country's manufacturing index fell for the 22nd consecutive month, with production of goods like televisions and radios down 38 percent year-on-year. Manufacturers are relocating to nations where labour is cheaper than in Thailand. In April 2015, production will cease at an [LG Electronics](/wiki/LG_Electronics "LG Electronics") factory in [Rayong Province](/wiki/Rayong_Province "Rayong Province").[[97]](#cite_note-97) Production is being moved to Vietnam, where labour costs per day are US$6.35 versus US$9.14 in Thailand. [Samsung Electronics](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") will site two large smartphone factories in Vietnam. It made around US$11 billion worth of investment pledges to the Vietnamese economy in 2014. As technologies evolve, e.g., as HDDs are replaced by [solid-state drives](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive") (SSDs), manufacturers are reexamining where best to produce these latest technologies.[[96]](#cite_note-Reuters-20150318-96) In addition, 74 percent of salaried workers in the sector face a high risk of being replaced by robots, as these positions consist of "repetitive, non-cognitive tasks".[[94]](#cite_note-ILO-201607-94): 39

#### Automotive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Automotive")]

Main article: [Automotive industry in Thailand](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Thailand "Automotive industry in Thailand")

Thailand is the [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") leader in automotive production and sales. The sector employed approximately 417,000 workers in 2015, representing 6.5 per cent of total employment across all manufacturing industries and accounting for roughly 10 percent of the country's GDP. In 2014, Thailand exported US$25.8 billion in automotive goods.[[94]](#cite_note-ILO-201607-94): 12–13 As many as 73 percent of automotive sector workers in Thailand face a high risk of job loss due to automation.[[94]](#cite_note-ILO-201607-94): xix

#### Gems and jewelry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Gems and jewelry")]

Gem and jewelry exports are Thailand's third-largest export category by value, trailing automotive and parts and computer components. In 2019, gem and jewelry exports, including gold, exceeded US$15.7 billion, up 30.3% from 2018 (486 billion baht, up 26.6%). Key export markets included [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), and [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"). The industry employs more than 700,000 workers according to the Gem and Jewelry Institute of Thailand (GIT).[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Energy")]

Further information: [Energy in Thailand](/wiki/Energy_in_Thailand "Energy in Thailand")

Thailand's 2004 energy consumption was estimated at 3.4 quadrillion British thermal units, representing about 0.7 percent of total [world energy consumption](/wiki/World_energy_consumption "World energy consumption"). Thailand is a net importer of oil and natural gas; however, the government is promoting ethanol to reduce imports of petroleum and the gasoline additive methyl tertiary butyl ether.

In 2005 Thailand's daily oil consumption of 838,000 barrels per day (133,200 m3/d) exceeded its production of 306,000 barrels per day (48,700 m3/d). Thailand's four oil refineries have a combined capacity of 703,100 barrels per day (111,780 m3/d). The government is considering a regional oil-processing and transportation hub serving south-central China. In 2004, Thailand's natural-gas consumption of 1,055 billion cubic feet (2.99×1010 m3) exceeded its production of 790 billion cubic feet (2.2×1010 m3).

Thailand's 2004 estimated coal consumption of 30.4 million short tons exceeded its production of 22.1 million. As of January 2007, [proven oil reserves](/wiki/Proven_reserves "Proven reserves") totaled 290 million barrels (46,000,000 m3) and proven natural-gas reserves were 14.8 trillion cubic feet (420 km3). In 2003, recoverable coal reserves totalled 1,493 million short tons.[[93]](#cite_note-cp-93)

In 2005, Thailand used about 118 billion [kilowatt hours](/wiki/Kilowatt_hour "Kilowatt hour") of electricity. Consumption rose by 4.7 percent in 2006, to 133 billion kWh. According to the [Electricity Generating Authority of Thailand](/wiki/Electricity_Generating_Authority_of_Thailand "Electricity Generating Authority of Thailand") (the national electricity utility), power consumption by residential users is increasing due to more favorable rates for residential customers than for the industry and business sectors. Thailand's electric utility and petroleum companies (also state-controlled) are being restructured.

### Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Services")]

In 2007 the service sector (which includes tourism, banking and finance), contributed 44.7 percent of GDP and employed 37 percent of the workforce.[[93]](#cite_note-cp-93) Thailand's service industry is competitive, contributing to its export growth.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Banking and finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Banking and finance")]

See also: [List of banks in Thailand](/wiki/List_of_banks_in_Thailand "List of banks in Thailand")

Dangerous levels of non-performing assets at Thai banks helped trigger an attack on the baht by currency speculators which led to the Asian financial crisis in 1997–1998. By 2003, nonperforming assets had been cut in half (to about 30 percent).

Despite a return to profitability, Thailand's banks continue to struggle with unrealized losses and inadequate capital. The government is considering reforms, including an integrated financial regulatory agency which would enable the [Bank of Thailand](/wiki/Bank_of_Thailand "Bank of Thailand") to focus on monetary policy. In addition, the government is attempting to strengthen the financial sector through the consolidation of commercial, state- and foreign-owned institutions. The 2004 Financial Sector Reform Master Plan provides tax breaks to financial institutions engaging in mergers and acquisitions. The reform program has been deemed successful by outside experts. In 2007 there were three state-owned commercial banks, five state-owned specialized banks, fifteen Thai commercial banks, and seventeen foreign banks in Thailand.[[93]](#cite_note-cp-93)

The Bank of Thailand sought to stem the flow of foreign funds into the country in December 2006, leading to the largest one-day drop in stock prices on the [Stock Exchange of Thailand](/wiki/Stock_Exchange_of_Thailand "Stock Exchange of Thailand") since the 1997 Asian financial crisis. The sell-off by foreign investors amounted to more than US$708 million.[[93]](#cite_note-cp-93)

In 2019, the Bank of Thailand kept its benchmark interest rate unchanged for a fourth straight meeting, with the concerns of high household debt and financial stability risks.[[99]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-99) As of 2025, Thailand's Monetary Policy Committee is expected to cut interest rates from 1.5%, likely two times by the first half of 2026, bringing the rate down to 1%.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

#### Retail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Retail")]

Retail employs more than 6 million Thai workers. Most are employed by small businesses. Large multinational and national retail players (such as [Lotus's](/wiki/Lotus%27s "Lotus's"), [7-Eleven](/wiki/7-Eleven "7-Eleven"), [Siam Makro](/wiki/Siam_Makro "Siam Makro"), [Big C](/wiki/Big_C "Big C"), Villa Market, [Central Group](/wiki/Central_Group "Central Group") and [The Mall Group](/wiki/The_Mall_Group "The Mall Group")) are estimated to employ fewer than 400,000 workers. This accounts for less than seven percent of Thailand's total employment in retail.[[94]](#cite_note-ILO-201607-94): 70

#### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Thailand](/wiki/Tourism_in_Thailand "Tourism in Thailand")

In 2016, tourism revenue, 2.53 trillion baht, accounted for 17.7 percent of Thailand's GDP, up from 16.7 percent in 2015. It is expected to generate 2.71 trillion baht in 2017. The global average for GDP contribution from tourism is nine percent.[[101]](#cite_note-BP-20170217-101)

**Cryptocurrencies**

[Thailand's Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(Thailand) "Ministry of Finance (Thailand)") approved four licensed brokers and dealers of cryptocurrencies in the country: Bx, [Bitkub](/wiki/Bitkub "Bitkub"), Coins and Satang Pro. The country still did not elaborated regulation for ICOs, though it announced in late 2018 to loosen the rules.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

## Labour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Labour")]

See also: [Thai labour law](/wiki/Thai_labour_law "Thai labour law")

Thailand's labour force has been estimated at from 36.8 million employed (of 55.6 million adults of working age)[[103]](#cite_note-BP-20160713-103) to 38.3 million (1Q2016).[[104]](#cite_note-NESDB-1Q2016-104) About 49 percent were employed in agriculture, 37 percent in the service sector and 14 percent in industry. In 2005 women constituted 48 percent of the labour force, and held an increased share of professional jobs. Thailand's unemployment rate was 0.9 percent as of 2014, down from two percent in 2004.[[39]](#cite_note-ILO-39) A [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") survey showed that 83.5 percent of the Thai workforce is unskilled.[[103]](#cite_note-BP-20160713-103)

A joint study by the Quality Learning Foundation (QLF), [Dhurakij Pundit University](/wiki/Dhurakij_Pundit_University "Dhurakij Pundit University") (DPU), and the World Bank suggests that 12 million Thais may lose their jobs to automation over the next 20 years, wiping out one-third of the positions in the workforce.[[103]](#cite_note-BP-20160713-103) The World Bank estimates that Thai workers are two times and five times less productive than Malaysian and Singaporean workers respectively. The report assesses the average output of Thai workers at US$25,000 (879,200 baht) in 2014 compared to Malaysia's US$50,000 and US$122,000 for Singapore.[[103]](#cite_note-BP-20160713-103) A 2016 report by the [International Labor Office](/wiki/International_Labor_Office "International Labor Office") (ILO) estimates that over 70 percent of Thai workers are in danger of being displaced by [automation](/wiki/Automation "Automation").[[94]](#cite_note-ILO-201607-94): xviii Factories in Thailand are estimated to be adding from 2,500 to 4,500 [industrial robots](/wiki/Industrial_robot "Industrial robot") per year.[[105]](#cite_note-105): 18

In fiscal year 2015, 71,000 Thais worked abroad in foreign countries. Taiwan employed the most Thai employees overall with 59,220 persons, followed by South Korea at 24,228, Israel at 23,479, Singapore at 20,000, and the UAE at 14,000. Most employees work in metal production, agriculture, textile manufacturing, and electronic part manufacturing fields.[[106]](#cite_note-106) As of 2020, Thai migrant labourers overseas generate remittances worth 140 billion baht.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

The number of migrant workers in Thailand is unknown. The official number—1,339,834 registered migrant workers from Cambodia, Laos, and Myanmar—reported by the Office of Foreign Workers Administration under the [Ministry of Labour](/wiki/Ministry_of_Labour_(Thailand) "Ministry of Labour (Thailand)"), represents only legal migrant workers. Many more are presumed to be non-registered or illegal migrants. The [Thailand Development Research Institute](/wiki/Thailand_Development_Research_Institute "Thailand Development Research Institute") (TDRI) estimates that there may yet be more illegal migrant workers than legal ones in Thailand.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

## Foreign trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Foreign trade")]

[![World map, showing Thai exports](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/2006Thai_exports.PNG/500px-2006Thai_exports.PNG)](/wiki/File:2006Thai_exports.PNG)

Thai export destinations, 2006.

China has replaced the United States as Thailand's largest export market while the latter still holds its position as its second-largest supplier (after [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")). While Thailand's traditional major markets have been [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), economic recovery in Thailand's regional trading partners has helped Thai export growth.

As of 2022, China is Thailand's largest trading partner with 3.69 trillion baht ($106 billion) worth of trade, accounting for almost 20% of all Thai foreign trade. In 2022, China also became the largest foreign investor in Thailand, totaling 77.4 billion baht, more than Japan and USA.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

Recovery from financial crisis depended heavily on increased exports to the rest of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). Since 2005 the increase in export of automobiles from Japanese manufacturers (particularly [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota"), [Nissan](/wiki/Nissan "Nissan") and [Isuzu](/wiki/Isuzu "Isuzu")) has helped improve the trade balance, with over one million cars produced annually since then. Thailand has joined the ranks of the world's top ten automobile-exporting nations.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Machinery and parts, [vehicles](/wiki/Vehicles "Vehicles"), integrated circuits, [chemicals](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry"), [crude oil](/wiki/Crude_oil "Crude oil"), fuels, [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") and [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") are among Thailand's principal imports. The increase in imports reflects a need to fuel production of high-tech items and vehicles.

Thailand is a member of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO), the Cairns Group of agricultural exporters and the [ASEAN Free Trade Area](/wiki/ASEAN_Free_Trade_Area "ASEAN Free Trade Area") (AFTA), and has pursued free-trade agreements. A China-Thailand Free Trade Agreement (FTA) began in October 2003. This agreement was limited to agricultural products, with a more comprehensive FTA planned to be signed by 2010. Thailand also has a limited free-trade agreement with India (since 2003) and a comprehensive Australia-Thailand Free Trade Agreement, which began on 1 January 2005.

Thailand began free trade negotiations with Japan in February 2004, and an in-principle agreement was agreed to in September 2005. Negotiations for a US-Thailand free trade agreement have been underway, with a fifth round of meetings held in November 2005.

Several industries are restricted to foreign investment by the 1999 Foreign Business Act. These industries include media, agriculture, distribution of land, professional services, tourism, hotels, and construction. Share ownership of companies engaged in these activities must be limited to a 49 percent minority stake. The 1966 US-Thailand Treaty of Amity and Economic Relations provides exemption of these restrictions for shareholders with United States citizenship.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

The [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") area is one of the most prosperous parts of Thailand and heavily dominates the national economy, with the infertile northeast being the poorest. A concern of successive Thai governments, and a focus of the recently ousted Thaksin government, has been to reduce the regional disparities which have been exacerbated by rapid economic growth in Bangkok and financial crisis.

Although little economic investment reaches other parts of the country except for tourist zones, the government has stimulated provincial economic growth in the [eastern seaboard](/wiki/Eastern_seaboard_of_Thailand "Eastern seaboard of Thailand") and the [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai") area. Despite talk of other regional development, these three regions and other tourist zones still dominate the national economy.

Although some US rights holders report good cooperation with Thai enforcement authorities (including the [Royal Thai Police](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Police "Royal Thai Police") and Royal Thai Customs), Thailand remained on the [priority watch list](/wiki/Office_of_the_United_States_Trade_Representative "Office of the United States Trade Representative") in 2012. The United States is encouraged that Thailand's government has affirmed its commitment to improving IPR protection and enforcement, but more must be done for Thailand to be removed from the list.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

Although the economy has grown moderately since 1999, future performance depends on continued reform of the financial sector, corporate-debt restructuring, attracting [foreign investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") and increasing exports. [Telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications"), roads, [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") generation and ports showed increasing strain during the period of sustained economic growth. Thailand is experiencing a growing shortage of engineers and skilled technical personnel.

## Regional economies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Regional economies")]

### Isan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Isan")]

Further information: [Economy of Isan](/wiki/Economy_of_Isan "Economy of Isan")

The economy of [Isan](/wiki/Isan "Isan") is dominated by [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), although output is poor and this sector is decreasing in importance at the expense of trade and the service sector. Most of the population is poor and badly educated. Many labourers have been driven by [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty") to seek work in other parts of Thailand or abroad.

Although Isan accounts for around a third of Thailand's population and a third of its area, it produces only 8.9 percent of [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"). Its economy grew at 6.2 percent per annum during the 1990s.

In 1995, 28 percent of the population was classed as below the poverty line, compared to just 7 percent in central Thailand. In 2000, [per capita](/wiki/Per_capita "Per capita") [income](/wiki/Income "Income") was 26,317 baht, compared to 208,434 in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"). Even within Isan, there is a [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural")/[urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") divide. In 1995, all of Thailand's ten poorest provinces were in Isan, the poorest being [Sisaket](/wiki/Sisaket_province "Sisaket province"). However, most wealth and [investment](/wiki/Investment_(macroeconomics) "Investment (macroeconomics)") is concentrated in the four major cities of [Khorat](/wiki/Nakhon_Ratchasima_Province "Nakhon Ratchasima Province"), [Ubon](/wiki/Ubon "Ubon"), [Udon](/wiki/Udon_Thani "Udon Thani"), and [Khon Kaen](/wiki/Khon_Kaen "Khon Kaen"). These four provinces account for 40 percent of the region's population.

### Special Economic Zones (SEZ)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Special Economic Zones (SEZ)")]

In his televised national address on 23 January 2015 in the program "Return Happiness to the People", Prime Minister [Prayut Chan-o-cha](/wiki/Prayut_Chan-o-cha "Prayut Chan-o-cha") addressed the government's policy on the establishment of [special economic zones](/wiki/Special_economic_zone "Special economic zone").[[114]](#cite_note-RTG-SEZ-114)

He said that the policy would promote connectivity and regional economic development on a sustainable basis. There are currently 10 [SEZs in Thailand](/wiki/List_of_Special_economic_zones#Thailand "List of Special economic zones"), with trade and investment valued at almost 800 billion baht a year.

In 2014, the government launched a pilot project to set up six special economic zones in five provinces: [Tak](/wiki/Tak_Province "Tak Province"), [Mukdahan](/wiki/Mukdahan_Province "Mukdahan Province"), [Sa Kaeo](/wiki/Sa_Kaeo_Province "Sa Kaeo Province"), [Songkhla](/wiki/Songkhla_Province "Songkhla Province"), and [Trat](/wiki/Trat_Province "Trat Province"). In the second phase, which is expected to begin in 2016, seven special economic zones will be established in another five provinces: [Chiang Rai](/wiki/Chiang_Rai_Province "Chiang Rai Province"), [Kanchanaburi](/wiki/Kanchanaburi_Province "Kanchanaburi Province"), [Nong Khai](/wiki/Nong_Khai_Province "Nong Khai Province"), [Nakhon Phanom](/wiki/Nakhon_Phanom_Province "Nakhon Phanom Province"), and [Narathiwat](/wiki/Narathiwat_Province "Narathiwat Province").[[114]](#cite_note-RTG-SEZ-114)

In early 2015, the government approved an infrastructure development plan in special economic zones. In 2015, the plan includes 45 projects, budgeted at 2.6 billion baht. Another 79 projects, worth 7.9 billion baht, will be carried out in 2016. Relying on a mix of government revenue, bond sales, and other funding, Prayut plans to spend US$83 billion over seven years on new railways, roads, and customs posts to establish cross-border trade routes. The idea is to link some 2.4 billion consumers in China and India with Asia's newest economic grouping, the [ASEAN Economic Community](/wiki/ASEAN_Economic_Community "ASEAN Economic Community"), of which Thailand is a member.[[115]](#cite_note-Mellor-20151109-115)

Critics of the SEZs maintain that free trade agreements and SEZs are incompatible with the principles of the late-King [Bhumibol](/wiki/Bhumibol "Bhumibol")'s [sufficiency economy](/wiki/Sufficiency_economy "Sufficiency economy"),[[116]](#cite_note-116) claimed by the government to be the inspiration for governmental economic and social policies.[[117]](#cite_note-MOE-2016-117)

## Shadow economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Economy_of_Thailand&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Shadow economy")]

"Thailand's shadow economy ranks globally among the highest," according to Friedrich Schneider, an economist at [Johannes Kepler University Linz](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler_University_Linz "Johannes Kepler University Linz") in Austria, author of *Hiding in the Shadows: The Growth of the Underground Economy*.[[118]](#cite_note-118) He estimates that Thailand's shadow economy accounted for about 40.9 percent of official GDP in 2014, a figure that includes [gambling](/wiki/Gambling_in_Thailand "Gambling in Thailand") and small-arms trafficking, but largely excludes narcotics.[[119]](#cite_note-BB-20150701-119) Schneider defines the shadow economy as market-based legal production that is intentionally concealed to avoid taxes, social-security payments, labour standards or administrative rules. His definition excludes purely illegal activities such as robbery, burglary or drug trafficking.[[120]](#cite_note-IZA-120) The shadow economy also encompasses widespread [loan sharking](/wiki/Loan_shark "Loan shark"). Government studies estimate around 200 000 informal lenders operate nationwide, many charging interest rates that place a heavy burden on low-income borrowers.[[121]](#cite_note-121)
